steps.text: Standing at the corner of Telegraph Avenue and Bancroft Way, it's easy to get caught up in the moment.
Throngs of busy undergrads walk to and fro while student clubs and political candidates vie for attention of passersby, all obscuring the rich history of the place.

steps.alt: A screenshot of a map of Telegraph Avenue
steps.text: Telegraph Avenue originated in the mid-1800s, gaining its name because it ran parallel to an early telegraph line. Today, it serves as a north-south artery of the East Bay, stretching from the southern edge of UC Berkeley to downtown Oakland. At the north end, Telegraph Avenue once stretched to Sather Gate–but it retreated to the corner of Bancroft Way in the 1950s when UC Berkeley expanded its campus.

steps.alt: A historic photo shows horse drawn carriages in Oakland
steps.text: In the 19th century, horse carriages rambled up and down the avenue, carrying students and everyone else where they needed to go.

steps.alt: Three men sit on a curb across from Moe's Bookstore
steps.text: In the 1960s and '70s, pictures of Telegraph Avenue were seen around the world as the street served as one of the iconic backdrops for a social movement…

steps.alt: A car outfitted with makeshift crowd control devices
steps.text: … and clashes between protesters and police.

With such a long history, change on Telegraph Avenue was inevitable. This project offers a glimpse of its transformations through the perspectives of small business owners who have worked on the street for decades. Studying these businesses, all of which are located in the northern section of Telegraph Avenue, offers readers an insight into the social and technological transformations of the past century.
